app.name: Glean
app.bestFor: Enterprise workplace search and RAG across many connected tools; strongest for broad cross-app knowledge retrieval.
app.rankBasis: Placed in all three cited enterprise knowledge and RAG roundups, with a 4.7/5 G2 rating from 163 reviews and 4.5/5 across more than 350 Gartner Peer Insights ratings in July 2026.
app.pricingPaid: Enterprise contract pricing — per-user licenses include pooled AI credits, with overage charges for advanced usage
pros: Reviewers consistently praise fast cross-application information retrieval
Permissions-aware indexing is built for complex enterprise estates
Broad connector coverage and polished user experience support company-wide adoption
cons: No public self-service price and procurement is enterprise-led
Connector rollout and permissions mapping require substantial administration
Value is harder to justify for smaller organizations with few data sources

app.name: Guru
app.bestFor: AI knowledge base that surfaces verified answers inside your workflow with source trust/verification built in.
app.rankBasis: Appears in all three cited AI knowledge-base roundups and has extensive independent review coverage, especially for verified knowledge and in-workflow answers.
app.pricingPaid: Custom enterprise package pricing — platform access, solution engineering, knowledge architecture, and ongoing optimization
pros: Users value answers appearing inside tools such as Slack and the browser
Verification workflows help teams assign ownership and freshness
Combines wiki, search, and intranet capabilities
cons: Current pricing is sales-led and less transparent than the former per-seat plans
Content verification creates an ongoing owner-maintenance burden
Search quality depends on disciplined source organization and connector setup

app.name: Notion AI
app.bestFor: Teams already in Notion who want RAG Q&A over their own docs and wiki without standing up separate infra.
app.rankBasis: Included in all three cited knowledge-management comparisons and benefits from Notion's large installed base, making it the lowest-friction AI knowledge layer for existing Notion workspaces.
app.pricingFree: Free and Plus plans include only trial AI capabilities
app.pricingPaid: Notion Business $20/member/month on monthly billing — full Notion AI, Enterprise Search, AI Meeting Notes, and agents
pros: Users can apply AI directly where their wiki and project content already lives
Flexible page and database model supports many knowledge workflows
Strong collaborative editing and low onboarding friction
cons: Full AI moved to the Business tier for new customers rather than a low-cost add-on
Flexible workspaces can become inconsistent and difficult to govern
Search quality falls when pages are duplicated, stale, or poorly structured

app.name: Confluence + Atlassian Intelligence
app.bestFor: Atlassian shops that want AI search and answers layered onto existing Confluence documentation.
app.rankBasis: Recommended in both cited enterprise knowledge-base roundups and backed by Confluence's large, mature review footprint among software and IT teams.
app.pricingFree: Free plan for up to 10 users with core pages, spaces, and templates
app.pricingPaid: Standard is sold per user with the published rate varying by team size and billing term — adds permissions, audit logs, and larger storage
pros: Reviewers value tight integration with Jira and established engineering workflows
Mature permissions, templates, and marketplace ecosystem
Strong fit when substantial documentation already lives in Confluence
cons: Large instances often accumulate stale pages and difficult navigation
Editing and information architecture can feel cumbersome
AI answer quality depends on documentation freshness and access configuration

app.name: Dashworks
app.bestFor: AI assistant that answers questions across all connected company apps without pre-indexing everything.
app.rankBasis: Appears in both cited enterprise knowledge roundups and earned positive early review signals for Slack-centric cross-app answers, but its independent-product signal weakened after HubSpot announced the acquisition in April 2025.
app.pricingPaid: Legacy Team, Business, and Enterprise plans were quote-based; standalone new-customer availability is no longer clearly published after the HubSpot acquisition
pros: Reviewers highlighted fast deployment without a large migration project
Strong Slack and browser workflow integration
Federated retrieval can surface current information across many systems
cons: Product roadmap and standalone availability are uncertain after the HubSpot acquisition
Public pricing is no longer transparent
Smaller review and ecosystem footprint than Glean, Guru, or Atlassian

app.name: CustomGPT.ai
app.bestFor: No-code RAG deployment with anti-hallucination controls; pitched at compliance-heavy and regulated orgs.
app.rankBasis: Included in both cited 2026 RAG-platform comparisons, but it has less independent review volume than the enterprise-search leaders and is primarily evaluated as a no-code custom chatbot builder.
app.pricingPaid: Standard paid tier supports 2 agents, 1 team member, and up to 5,000 documents per agent; the current dollar price is shown on the official checkout
pros: Users value quick deployment without building a RAG stack
Source citations and controlled content are useful for customer-facing answers
Broad ingestion formats and straightforward embedding
cons: No standing free plan and lower tiers impose agent, document, and team limits
Less suitable for broad permission-aware enterprise search across live applications
Independent review volume is modest relative to larger knowledge platforms

skill.name: LangChain
skill.bestFor: The de-facto framework for building RAG and knowledge-base apps — chains, agents, and connectors to every model and vector store. The category's recognized leader (~142k stars).
skill.rankBasis: The existing source set records roughly 142k GitHub stars and places LangChain in the top tier of RAG frameworks, giving it the broadest developer mindshare in this track.
pros: Largest integration ecosystem among general LLM application frameworks
Extensive examples, community support, and provider coverage
Flexible enough for highly customized RAG architectures
cons: Rapid API evolution and abstraction churn can complicate upgrades
Many layers make debugging harder than using provider SDKs directly
Production reliability, evaluation, and infrastructure remain the user's responsibility

skill.name: Dify
skill.bestFor: Open-source LLM app platform with a built-in knowledge base, document ingestion, and RAG pipeline behind a full UI; the highest-starred option here (~150k stars).
skill.rankBasis: The existing GitHub source records roughly 150k stars, the largest raw star signal in this file, and Dify is widely compared as a full open-source alternative to commercial LLM app builders.
skill.pricingPaid: Optional Dify Cloud plans provide managed hosting with message, storage, and team limits
pros: Full UI makes RAG application building accessible beyond developers
Combines ingestion, workflows, model access, and deployment in one platform
Large and active open-source community
cons: Self-hosting involves multiple services and nontrivial upgrades
Complex workflows can become difficult to version and debug
Cloud and model usage limits add cost beyond the open-source license

skill.name: RAGFlow
skill.bestFor: Dedicated open-source RAG engine with deep document parsing, knowledge-base management, and a zero-code web UI (~86k stars).
skill.rankBasis: The existing GitHub source records roughly 86k stars, the strongest adoption signal among dedicated document-centric RAG engines in this category.
features: Deep document parsing and layout analysis
Visual RAG and agent workflow UI
pros: Users highlight strong parsing of complex PDFs and structured documents
End-to-end UI reduces the amount of custom ingestion code
Retrieval components remain configurable for specialist corpora
cons: Deployment is resource-heavy and operationally complex
Documentation and upgrade experience can lag the project's rapid development
Production quality still depends on model, parser, and retrieval tuning

skill.name: AnythingLLM
skill.bestFor: All-in-one desktop/self-hosted app that turns your documents into a private chat knowledge base out of the box (~64k stars).
skill.rankBasis: The existing source records roughly 64k GitHub stars, making AnythingLLM one of the most adopted turnkey private-document chat applications rather than a developer-only framework.
pros: Reviewers value the fast path from installation to private document chat
Supports local models for privacy-sensitive use cases
All-in-one interface avoids assembling a RAG stack manually
cons: Retrieval quality on complex corpora needs manual tuning
Desktop simplicity gives way to infrastructure work for multi-user deployment
Advanced governance and evaluation are thinner than enterprise platforms

skill.name: LlamaIndex
skill.bestFor: The data framework for indexing and querying your private content; the reference toolkit for document-QA knowledge bases (~51k stars).
skill.rankBasis: The existing GitHub source records roughly 51k stars, and developer comparisons consistently identify LlamaIndex as a reference framework for connecting private data to LLMs.
skill.pricingPaid: Optional LlamaCloud provides managed parsing, extraction, indexing, and retrieval on usage-based plans
pros: Purpose-built data abstractions cover many RAG ingestion patterns
Broad connector and vector-store ecosystem
Strong documentation and examples for document-QA systems
cons: Large API surface and frequent evolution create a learning burden
Choosing among overlapping abstractions can be confusing
Managed parsing and production services introduce additional usage cost

skill.name: obsidian-skills (kepano)
skill.bestFor: Official Obsidian Agent Skills that teach an agent to read/write your Markdown vault, Bases, and Canvas as a knowledge base (~43k stars).
skill.rankBasis: The existing source records roughly 43k GitHub stars, the largest adoption signal among Obsidian-specific agent-skill projects, and it is maintained by Obsidian's CEO.
pros: Maintainer provenance makes it the clearest reference for Obsidian file formats
Plain-text skills preserve local ownership and portability
Covers newer Obsidian primitives beyond ordinary Markdown notes
cons: Provides agent instructions rather than a complete search or RAG engine
Safe operation still depends on the agent respecting vault structure
Obsidian-specific conventions limit use outside that ecosystem

skill.name: Quivr
skill.bestFor: Opinionated RAG 'second brain' you can plug into your apps to store and query your documents (~39k stars).
skill.rankBasis: The existing source records roughly 39k GitHub stars, a strong adoption signal for an opinionated open-source second-brain and RAG backend.
pros: Provides more application structure than a low-level RAG library
Open-source backend can be embedded into custom products
Supports multiple model and storage choices
cons: Project direction and documentation have changed substantially across versions
Self-hosting requires database, model, and worker operations
Smaller integration and support ecosystem than LangChain or LlamaIndex

skill.name: Khoj
skill.bestFor: Self-hostable AI second brain that searches and chats across your notes, docs, and PDFs (~36k stars).
skill.rankBasis: The existing source records roughly 36k GitHub stars, placing Khoj among the most adopted self-hostable personal knowledge assistants.
skill.pricingPaid: Optional hosted Khoj service offers managed access and higher usage limits
features: Semantic search across notes and documents
pros: Users value local-first integrations with existing note workflows
Can combine private documents with web and model tools
Self-hosting preserves control over personal knowledge data
cons: Setup and model configuration are more involved than a consumer note app
Best experience depends on specific editor integrations
Small-team governance is limited compared with enterprise knowledge platforms

skill.name: Haystack (deepset)
skill.bestFor: deepset's modular, production-grade pipelines for search and RAG over your knowledge base (~26k stars).
skill.rankBasis: The existing source records roughly 26k GitHub stars, and Haystack has a long-standing production reputation in open-source search and RAG pipelines.
pros: Reviewers and developers value explicit, testable pipeline composition
Strong fit for production search systems and custom retrieval logic
Mature project backed by deepset and an active community
cons: More engineering-oriented than turnkey knowledge-base products
Major-version migration requires meaningful code changes
Users must operate storage, models, monitoring, and scaling themselves

skill.name: claude-obsidian (AgriciDaniel)
skill.bestFor: Self-organizing second-brain skill set: drop any source and Claude reads, links, and files it into one connected Markdown knowledge graph (~9.9k stars).
skill.rankBasis: The existing GitHub source records roughly 9.9k stars, a meaningful community signal for a specialist skill set but well below the general RAG platforms ranked above it.
pros: Opinionated automation reduces manual filing work
Plain Markdown output avoids proprietary storage
Useful end-to-end workflow for turning mixed inputs into linked notes
cons: Autonomous reorganizing can create unwanted links or file changes
Requires Claude tooling and careful vault backups
Independent review evidence is limited relative to its star count

skill.name: obsidian-second-brain (eugeniughelbur)
skill.bestFor: Cross-CLI skill turning a vault into an AI-first knowledge base with hybrid semantic search and self-maintaining notes (~3.5k stars).
skill.rankBasis: The existing source records roughly 3.5k GitHub stars, showing credible niche interest in an AI-first Obsidian workflow but substantially less adoption than the leading vault and RAG projects.
pros: Hybrid search addresses limitations of filename or keyword lookup alone
Cross-agent design avoids locking the workflow to one CLI
Markdown vault remains portable and user-controlled
cons: Smaller community means fewer independent reliability signals
Embedding and index setup add local complexity
Automated note maintenance can make surprising changes without strict review

notes: Category is contaminated by vendor blogs that rank their own product #1 (e.g. CustomGPT.ai, Chitika) — those are weighted down in the app track. The Skill/Repo track is now ranked by real quality, reputation, and adoption (GitHub stars) rather than form factor — kind ('skill' vs 'repo') is only a tag, so a hugely-adopted RAG repo outranks a niche skill. It mixes the notable open-source RAG/knowledge-base frameworks (LangChain, Dify, RAGFlow, AnythingLLM, LlamaIndex, Quivr, Khoj, Haystack) with genuine Agent Skills for a personal knowledge base (kepano's official Obsidian skills and the Obsidian second-brain skill family). Star counts verified via the GitHub API (Jul 2026).
